title: Cross-Merchant Synthetic Identity Fragment Mosaic Attack
narrative: Fraudster constructs synthetic identity by strategically spreading identity components across 8-15 merchants over 45-90 days, each merchant seeing partial data that appears legitimate in isolation. First wave uses email/billing address at low-risk MCCs (5411 grocery, 5912 pharmacy) with card1_txn_count_7d = 1-2, amounts $18-$47. Second wave introduces shipping address at MCC 5732/5734 electronics retailers, dist1 values 850-2200km from billing, amounts climbing to $120-$280. Final bust-out phase hits 3-5 high-value merchants (MCC 5399, 5541) within 72 hours, card1_txn_count_24h spikes to 7-12, card1_amt_zscore exceeds 3.8, all using the now-established email/address combination. The synthetic's addr1 appears valid at each merchant independently, but cross-merchant analysis reveals no transaction history predating the 90-day window and impossible geographic velocity between dist1 calculations across the merchant sequence.
indicators: card1 has transactions at 8+ distinct MIDs within 90 days with card1_txn_count_lifetime < 95 days
dist1 values between consecutive transactions exceed 800km for 40%+ of transaction pairs
card1_txn_count_7d increases from 1-2 to 7-12 within final 72-hour period
P_emaildomain and addr1 combination has zero transaction history beyond 90-day lookback window
MCC progression moves from low-risk (5411, 5912, 5541) to high-risk (5399, 5732) categories within 60 days
card1_amt_zscore spikes from 0.3-0.9 baseline to 3.5+ in final transaction cluster
TransactionAmt escalates from $18-$47 range to $240-$890 range across merchant sequence
summary: A payment card appears at 8+ different merchants over 90 days with no prior history, showing impossible geographic jumps (800+ km between purchases) and a sudden spike from 1-2 weekly transactions to 7-12 in 24 hours. The email and address combination exists nowhere in historical data, and spending escalates from small grocery purchases to high-value electronics in the final 72 hours.
reasoning: The fraudster is building credibility for a fake identity by scattering small, normal-looking purchases across many merchants before the final cash-out. Real customers have transaction history beyond 90 days and don't teleport between distant locations or suddenly change their spending behavior this drastically—this coordinated escalation signals a planned synthetic identity bust-out.
indicator_explanations: Card hits many different merchants rapidly despite being brand new
Transactions ping-pong across impossible geographic distances between purchases
Card usage explodes from occasional to frenzied in final days
Email and address pairing appears freshly fabricated with no purchase history
Spending pattern shifts from groceries to electronics liquidation merchants
Final transactions are drastically larger than card's established spending pattern
Purchase amounts escalate from small tests to high-value cashout attempts
